coredata.dc:title: Green supercapacitors: Latest developments and perspectives in the pursuit of sustainability
coredata.dc:creator: Muzaffar, Aqib
Ahamed, M. Basheer
Hussain, Chaudhery Mustansar
coredata.dc:description: The slogans of modern times, “go green,” referring to the development of sustainable energy sources, have served as the main pushing force for the exploration of clean and green energy alternatives. Green energy sources are portrayed to limit the worldwide dependency on fossil fuels for energy supply, keeping in view their hazardous impact on the environment. Supercapacitors are electrochemical devices using the principle of electrochemical conversions for energy storage, providing a cleaner, greener and sustainable energy storing and delivering system. However, exploring the design aspects to develop such green energy alternatives remains essential and central. The understanding of designing aspects portrays their applicability. Apart from designing such devices, the green energy or eco-friendliness of such devices prior and after their usage cannot be neglected. The main aim behind exploration of supercapacitors is the elevation of energy density without compromising to the high power density capability. The elevation in energy density of supercapacitors can fill the void between batteries and fuel cells, thereby enabling sustainable energy storing devices. Furthermore, the elevation in specific capacitance to 1000–10000 F for supercapacitors can enhance their applicability in modern times. The enhancements in energy density and specific capacitance can play a vital role in overshadowing the limitations of batteries and fuel cells, thereby marking significance of supercapacitors among the electrochemical energy storing devices. The importance of greener and cleaner energy sources in the development of sustainable energy devices stays fundamental to this review. This review attempts to elaborate on the design aspects of green supercapacitors and the different green materials explored for supercapacitor applications in recent times to compete against the energy storing devices.

head.ce:title: Green supercapacitors: Latest developments and perspectives in the pursuit of sustainability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The slogans of modern times, “go green,” referring to the development of sustainable energy sources, have served as the main pushing force for the exploration of clean and green energy alternatives. Green energy sources are portrayed to limit the worldwide dependency on fossil fuels for energy supply, keeping in view their hazardous impact on the environment. Supercapacitors are electrochemical devices using the principle of electrochemical conversions for energy storage, providing a cleaner, greener and sustainable energy storing and delivering system. However, exploring the design aspects to develop such green energy alternatives remains essential and central. The understanding of designing aspects portrays their applicability. Apart from designing such devices, the green energy or eco-friendliness of such devices prior and after their usage cannot be neglected. The main aim behind exploration of supercapacitors is the elevation of energy density without compromising to the high power density capability. The elevation in energy density of supercapacitors can fill the void between batteries and fuel cells, thereby enabling sustainable energy storing devices. Furthermore, the elevation in specific capacitance to 1000–10000 F for supercapacitors can enhance their applicability in modern times. The enhancements in energy density and specific capacitance can play a vital role in overshadowing the limitations of batteries and fuel cells, thereby marking significance of supercapacitors among the electrochemical energy storing devices. The importance of greener and cleaner energy sources in the development of sustainable energy devices stays fundamental to this review. This review attempts to elaborate on the design aspects of green supercapacitors and the different green materials explored for supercapacitor applications in recent times to compete against the energy storing devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The prime goal of the research community in recent times has been the development of devices capable of harvesting energy from renewable energy sources. The urgency in developing efficient energy harvesting devices arises due to energy scarcity and rising energy demand. Adjacently, there have been numerous attempts to develop alternative methods dedicated to energy storage in the form of electrical energy. In this context, supercapacitors present an efficient means for storing electrical energy based on the principle of electrochemical conversion. Supercapacitors are often referred to as intermediate energy-storing devices that narrow the void between batteries and traditional capacitors [1–3]. Supercapacitors exhibit merits like enhanced power and energy density, rapid and simultaneous high current discharge, low cost and maintenance, least pollution, and environmental friendliness [4–10]. These merits have enabled the development of supercapacitors featuring a high power supply in contrast to batteries.

The worldwide energy demand is predicted to double in the next two decades [11]. This fact is evident based on energy's role in improving our living standards. The role of energy stays central to social and economic developments [12]. Modern economies are currently formulated on the availability of reliable and competent energy sources and devices. The depleting rate of conventional energy sources based on fossil fuels, the environmental pollution, and the threat to ecosystems caused by their use requires the development of unconventional energy sources. The primary claim concerned with energy harvesting from fossil fuels is long-term sustainability. This concern has shifted focus towards the development of renewable and sustainable energy harvesting technologies on research and industrial levels. The idea has been gaining a remarkable amount of interest since the beginning of the 21st century. Mostly, the attention has been diverted toward efficient energy harvesting from sources like solar, wind, thermal, and biofuels. Nevertheless, in the past decade, the focus has shifted significantly towards energy storage and harvesting by the electrochemical means like rechargeable batteries and supercapacitors. The overwhelming attention towards developing sustainable electrochemical devices is dedicated to energy storage and harvesting has led to the evolution of hybrid electric vehicles, smart grids, portable electronics, etc.

The supercapacitors feature in the energy-storing and harvesting technologies dedicated to deliver energy at a rapid rate with a high current offering for a short duration. Their fast energy delivery rate marks their strong candidature in hybrid electric vehicles, uninterrupted power supplies, and memory backup. In addition, supercapacitors exhibit unlimited cyclic life with higher specific power, low-temperature charge, discharge kinetics, etc.

Supercapacitors usually follow three basic principles based on the energy storing mechanism. Then energy-storing principles include an electrochemical double-layer capacitor (EDLC), pseudocapacitor and a combination of both termed as hybrid supercapacitors. The first type of supercapacitor (EDLC) is characterized by the formation of the Helmholtz double layer at the electrode-electrolyte boundaries causing fast adsorption-desorption of the electrolyte ions yielding high power density without involving a faradaic process. The factors having a significant impact on double-layer capacitance are the area of the electrode surface and the thickness of the Helmholtz double layer. For double-layer capacitors, highly porous materials like activated carbon, carbon nanosheets, carbon nanotubes, and graphene are commonly used [13].

The second type of supercapacitor, called pseudocapacitor, is based on fast and reversible faradaic redox reactions at the interface between the electrode and electrolyte. The reactions occur at the electrode surface, yielding higher energy density, shorter cyclic life, and lower rate capability [14]. In contrast to the double-layer capacitor, pseudocapacitors operate at relatively low voltage and suffer from lower energy density. For pseudocapacitors, metal sulfides and conducting polymers are mostly used. Finally, the hybrid supercapacitors are essentially formed as the combination of double-layer capacitors and pseudocapacitors utilizing the high power feature of a double-layer capacitor and high energy density of pseudocapacitors to yield excellent cyclic stability along with better electrochemical performance.

One of the main concerns in modern times is the rising environmental pollution due to serious and hazardous consequences. Electrical energy remains at the core of handling the issue of environmental pollution, so its applicability in various transportation means has recently gained considerable attention. Essentially the energy tendered using conventional sources stays limited to feed the current demands along with their consistent price hike. The focus has shifted towards developing energy sources utilizing green and sustainable materials. The challenging factor for employing green and sustainable energy materials is the fulfillment of the desired energy requirements. The designing perspective of next-generation green supercapacitors is essential regarding their sustainability. It formulates the main challenging feature in their broader exploration and applicability. The designing aspect for green supercapacitors has been hindering their exploration to the fullest potential. The green and clean energy alternatives have to offer to meet the promise. Another important aspect apart from the designing of green supercapacitors is their eco-friendliness. This aspect highlights the promising potential of new and novel materials to be used as clean and sustainable energy alternatives to replace the current non-renewable energy sources.

Most of the reviews on supercapacitors are mainly focused on the innovations leading to enhanced energy density of the devices to elevate their applicability. The energy density parameter enhancement remains pivotal in developing efficient supercapacitors. However, the environmental concerns associated with the development of supercapacitors also require due attention. The approaches involved in developing green supercapacitors, keeping in view the energy density parameter, stay new in research and development. It is also essential to ascertain the importance of green materials, biodegradable materials, and bio-waste materials in development of supercapacitors along with green fabrication techniques. The application of green materials for supercapacitors stays fresh as per recent trends in supercapacitors. This review aims to provide insights into green supercapacitor technology, portraying their design aspects, challenges, current status, and future trends and establishing the importance of green energy devices in the upcoming decades.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Perspective in the design of modern energy storing devices green supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Energy consumption has risen globally and has shown an exponential rise since the industrial revolution [15]. The rise in energy consumption has dramatically influenced the quality standards of life to a significant extent. Currently, most energy requirements globally are met by the combustion of fossil fuels, thereby marking their presence as an essential and integral part of the present world. The energy tendered from renewable sources like solar, wind, geothermal, hydro, tidal, etc., stays limited; hence, the search for efficient energy harvesting and robust devices remains a central focus for research and development. The need and significance of the green supercapacitors is expressed in Fig. 1 .

The extensive dependence on fossil fuels for energy requirements has resulted in enormous environmental pollution, due to which serious threats are posed to the existence of humans and other forms of life on earth in addition to environmental degradation. The continuous use of fossil fuels contributes significantly to the increase in CO2 concentrations in the atmosphere and thereby intensifying the perspective of global warming and climatic changes. The health and environmental concerns caused by the usage of fossil fuels are not the only reasons to search for new energy alternatives. The rate of depletion of fossil fuels and the impact on economies has rung alarming bells to call for alternative energy sources to fulfill the primary energy needs. The development of novel energy technologies dedicated to energy conversion and harvesting with a high degree of efficiency and minimal polluting factors has been actively parallel to this cause. The research and development of novel energy sources should focus on minimal to no pollutant emission, versatility, compatibility, applicability, and sustainability. The concerns over environmental pollution have paved a new way for developing green energy sources with the features mentioned earlier [16,17].

Energy consumption is necessary for modern civilization to avail the comfort and convenience of life. The quantity of energy consumption has been portrayed as the standard indicator for the grade of living along with the degree of industrialization. In the past few decades, excessive daily energy consumption has raised adverse environmental consequences, thereby causing the local and global environment [18]. It is essential to highlight here that the energy output from different energy sources is different; hence, they display diverse degrees of environmental impact. For instance, the negative impact of fossil fuels on the environment is a well-known fact. At the same time, the environmental impact of considering renewable sources is also considerably lower than that of fossil fuels. The rising impact of using excessive energy from renewable or bioenergy sources have also been felt. The hike indicates the negative impact of excessive bio-energy usage on food product prices over the last two decades. Hence, it stays central to study the composition of energy systems and their impact on the environment, society, and economy before their up-scale applicability.

Despite displaying high specific capacitance, Supercapacitors face challenges in energy density, which constrains their fullest potential to be used as energy storage devices instead of rechargeable batteries. In supercapacitors, the energy density is directly proportional to the specific capacitance and the square of the operational voltage. Thus, to improve the energy density factor of a supercapacitor, either specific capacitance or operational voltage or both parameters require significant enhancements. One of the most common methods to attain higher energy density is to use electrode materials with higher specific capacitance and electrolytes with stable and higher potential windows. The other method involves system integrity optimization to attain improved outcomes.

The designing and fabrication of supercapacitor electrodes are instead very facile. However, their operation and synchronization to yield the desired outcome are challenging. Therefore, it is essential to balance the surface morphology of the electrode material with the electrolytic ions. Electrolytes play a vital role in establishing the potential window, defined as the limit within which the supercapacitor is functional or operational. The electrolytes, in addition to that, are responsible for the ionic conductivity and operational temperature window as well. Hence, while designing green supercapacitors, the electrolytic consideration includes higher ionic concentrations, electrochemical stability, lower values for equivalent series resistance and volatility, and lower viscosity and toxicity [19,20].

The other design perspective required to improve the specific capacitance of green supercapacitors is to insert pseudocapacitance materials within the double-layer framework. By incorporating pseudocapacitance structures into double-layer structures, the generation of faradaic current occurs. The chemical affinity of electrode ions is raised, thereby yielding higher values for specific capacitance [21]. The designing perspectives for green supercapacitors using nanostructured materials can be classified into zero, one, two and three dimensional nanostructures, as shown in Fig. 2 . The design classification for supercapacitors in terms of nanomaterials presents a wide variety of materials for exploration.

The morphology and structure of the electrode material also play a vital role in providing pseudocapacitance. However, the main criteria to exhibit pseudocapacitance are the strong affinity towards redox reactions or excellent redox activity [22–24]. To produce green supercapacitors, the materials designated for supercapacitors behaviour can be designed as zero-dimensional nanostructures, one-dimensional supercapacitors, and two-dimensional, three-dimensional, hierarchical and core-shell structures. These design paradigms are elaborated on in the following sections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zero dimensional nanostructures for green supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: The nanostructures in the form of spheres (more or less) including quantum dots, fullerenes, nanoparticles, nano-onions, etc are designed as zero-dimensional nanostructures. Zero-dimensional nanostructures are generally defined as the nanoparticles constrained within a nanoscale ranging from 0 to 100 nm usually having three dimensions [25]. The zero-dimensional nanostructures designed for supercapacitors follow solid, hollow and core-shell structures. For supercapacitor electrodes, the solid nanoparticles or nanospheres are considered the basis of zero-dimensional nanostructures. In this context, the different forms of carbon like activated carbon, carbon nanospheres, mesoporous carbon and transition metal oxides procured via green synthesis protocols have been reported as solid nanoparticles for green supercapacitors [26–30]. The activated carbon has found a definite and distinctive place due to its higher surface area with economical and easy fabrication. The activated carbons exhibit a wide range of pore distribution and are grouped into categories viz; micropores, mesopores and macropores, ideal for double layer capacitance. In this regard, onion-like activated carbon nanostructures have been reported with a specific surface area of (nearly) 500 m2 g−1 prepared by annealing [31]. The nanostructured activated carbon anions displayed an excellent rate capability of (nearly) 70% in the scan rate of 1–100 mV s−1 with a relaxation time constant of 26 m s.

The zero-dimensional nanostructures have also found applicability in the fabrication of supercapacitors via green synthesis routes. However, the transition metal-based supercapacitor demonstrates pseudocapacitance behavior instead of double-layer capacitance behaviour, relying on surface-based redox reactions. The metal oxide-based nanostructures reported as green supercapacitors or synthesized following green synthesis routes have demonstrated comparatively better electrical conductivity than the micro or macro-structured particles. The effect of the electrolyte by varying the anionic concentration and the type of electrolytes also significantly impacts the electrochemical performance of green supercapacitors. Thus, exploiting the electrode designated for green supercapacitors along with the electrolyte's impact results in better charge storage. Hence, to maximize charge storage, along with traditional zero-dimensional materials for green supercapacitors, the addition of materials like perovskite, etc can provide new insights into research and development of novel and eco-friendly supercapacitors. These types of structures formulate solid zero-dimensional nanostructures.

In addition to solid nanostructures in zero-dimensional nanostructures, the hollow zero-dimensional nanostructure design approach has also been recognized as a promising option for designing green supercapacitors. The hollow zero-dimensional nanostructure design presents peculiar properties like low density, shorter pathways for ionic transportation along with higher surface-to-volume ratios, etc [32]. The zero-dimensional nanostructures for green supercapacitors can be prepared using hard templating, template free, and soft template methods. Among the three, the hard templating method has been widely explored and adapted for supercapacitor fabrication due to various advantages like shape, size, and structural optimizations in the final product [33]. Generally, electrodes in the form of hollow single-shelled structures are prepared by coating a thin layer of precursor material on a spherical-shaped hard template composed of silica, polystyrene, or carbon. After coating the precursor material, the template is removed using chemical etching or calcination. In this context, the hard templating method has reported hollow carbonaceous nanospheres by using carbonization to yield hollow carbon nanospheres on silica [34]. The hollow zero-dimensional carbonaceous electrodes demonstrated a high specific surface area of 1704 m2 g−1, porosity in the range of 6.4-3.1 m with a pore volume of 1.6 cm3 g−1. The hollow nanostructure design displayed a specific capacitance of 251 F g−1 at 50 mV s−1. The other materials reported for hollow nanostructures are MnO2, NiO, and NiS [35–37]. NiO, in particular, prepared by layer-layer, self-assembly approach as hollow nanospheres, as shown in Fig. 3 has revealed great promise as zero-dimensional nanostructures [36]. The method employed involved cycles of immersion and precipitation process to formulate shells as shown in Fig. 3 (a) prior to removal of carbonaceous template by calcination. By repeated immersion and precipitation multiple times, mono, bi, and tri-shelled NiO hollow spheres were attained, as shown in Fig. 3 (b and d). Among these NiO hollow nanospheres, bi-shelled nanospheres displayed a maximum surface area of 92.00 m2 g−1 due to loosely packed structure, in contrast to the mono and the tri-shelled structures. The bi-shelled nanospheres, as such, displayed a maximum specific capacitance of 612.5 F g−1.

Not only have single-shelled hollow zero-dimensional nanostructures been reported for green synthesis of supercapacitors, but multi-shell hollow nanostructures have also been investigated. The multi-shell zero-dimensional nanostructures display fascinating properties like improved surface-to-volume ratio to provide a feasible and an efficient design approach for supercapacitors. In this regard, the electrode materials composed of Co3O4, Fe2O3, and NiO have been successfully fabricated as multi-shell zero-dimensional nanostructures [38–40]. The synthesis of NiO hollow nanospheres following a layer-by-layer approach and self-assembly fabrication has been reported for supercapacitor electrodes [40]. The synthesis involved direct immersion and precipitation prior to the removal of the carbon-based template by calcination. The hollow nanospheres exhibited a surface area of 92 m2 g−1, portraying a porous or loosely packed structure. Among the multi-layered NiO nanospheres, the double-layered NiO nanospheres exhibit exceptional electrochemical performance with a specific capacitance of 612.5 F g−1.

In addition, solid and hollow zero-dimensional core-shell structures involve coating solid or hollow nanoparticles on a thin shell. The core-shell designing approach combines faradaic and non-faradaic materials into zero-dimensional nanostructures to present advantages like improved electrical conductivity, least amalgamation, and robust mechanical and chemical stability [40–42].

In this context, conducting polymer (polyaniline) has been coated on carbon nanospheres as supercapacitor electrodes [43]. The hollow carbon nanospheres provided an exceptionally high surface area (2239 m2 g−1). They were synthesized using ferrocene precursor for carbon preparation along with silica spheres with the template. Polyaniline was then grown on the carbonaceous surface or hollow carbon nanospheres at room temperature at an optimized polyaniline concentration. The core-shell zero-dimensional nanostructure demonstrated a specific capacitance of 525 F g−1. The overwhelming electrochemical performance is attributed to the high porosity presented by the calcareous shell allowing easy transportation. Table 1 summarizes the most explored materials in the form of zero-dimensional nanostructures for supercapacitors.

A zero-dimensional supercapacitor using carbon quantum dots/CoS2 nanocomposite (CQDs/CoS2) was recently synthesized through a solvent-free ball milling route followed by solvothermal treatment [41]. The preparation comprised green catalyst-free microwave-assisted process from glycerol, as a single precursor, with a quantum yield of ∼16%, followed by a ball mill–hydrothermal combination method. The synthesized CQDs/CoS2 exhibited edge bricklike morphology with a large surface area of 427.2 m2g-1. The nanocomposite exhibited excellent conductivity and high mesoporosity. In terms of electrochemical properties, the nanocomposite demonstrated pseudocapacitance of 808 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1 with higher rate capability of 83.9%, corresponding to a retention rate of 20 A g−1. The nanocomposite-based supercapacitors exhibited cyclic stability of 98.75% over 10000 charging/discharging cycles, thus portraying the nanocomposite supercapacitor as a green energy storing device.

The one-dimensional nanostructures for supercapacitor electrode design are considered due to unique physical and chemical features [44]. The reason for considering one-dimensional nanostructure for supercapacitor electrodes is the efficient transportation of ions and electrons while retaining similar benefits to those in zero-dimensional configurations, constrained to two dimensions on the nanoscale. The one-dimensional nanostructure design is either homostructure or heterostructure. The one-dimensional homostructure includes structures with nanorod, nanotube, or nanowire configurations classified based on aspect ratio. The synthesis approach for developing a one-dimensional homostructure is unique and versatile, including template-free and template-assisted methods. For template-free methods, hydrothermal, solvothermal, electrodeposition, etc., synthesis routes are employed, while for template-assisted methods, sacrificial templates are commonly employed to develop initial structures followed by template removal by applying acidic or basic treatment. In addition, top-down or bottom-up approaches have also been employed in developing or growing vertically aligned active electrode materials in conductive substrates. These approaches in designing supercapacitor electrodes guarantee efficient and easy transport for electrons from the current collector to the electrode material due to binder-free designing and allow faster ionic transfer among the electrode and electrolyte attributed to the free spaces available in the nanostructure.

The one-dimensional nanopillar design formulates an attempt to produce self-sustaining supercapacitor electrode structures with lower chances to collapse. As such one-dimensional nanopillar structures become more accessible to the electrolyte, yielding excellent ionic conductivity. In this context, oxygen-deficient Fe2O3 nanorods have been fabricated as supercapacitor electrodes [45]. The electrodes were prepared following the hydrothermal method using an aqueous solution with ferrous chloride, sodium nitrate, and hydrogen chloride as the precursors. The FeOOH-based nanorods were separately heated at a high temperature in air and N2 environment to yield pristine Fe2O3 nanorods along with oxygen-deficient nanorods. Among the two fabricated nanorods, the latter displayed overwhelming electrochemical characteristics with a specific capacitance of 64.5 F g−1. The higher electrochemical response in the nanorod structure is attributed to the vacancies provided due to oxygen deficiency to enhance the conductivity and reactivity of the nanorods.

The one-dimensional design perspective is elaborated to nanotube and nanowire structures as homostructural designing paradigms. The nanotubular structure provides more surface area with low mass utilization to yield better gravimetric capacitance. In contrast, the nanowire structure provides a higher aspect ratio to facilitate a large surface area for charge storage and efficient routes for charge transport. The most promising nanotube structure for charge storage is the carbon nanotubes; specifically vertically aligned ones formulated using the direct deposition method. Carbon nanotubes as supercapacitor electrodes provide a surface area of 120–500 m2 g−1, a porous inner structure, and extraordinary thermal and mechanical stability. In this regard, previously reported vertically aligned carbon nanotubes (400 μm in length) were directly grown on metallic alloy using a vapour-phase catalyst delivery system [46]. The cyclic voltammetry pattern of the vertically aligned carbon nanotubes stays symmetric at low and higher scan rates determining its excellent capacitive behaviour. The carbon nanotubes showed low values for contact resistance between them and the metallic alloy. The fabricated nanotube structure exhibited a specific capacitance of 18 F g−1. Considering the nanostructures spinal nickel cobaltite (NiCo2O4) as a ternary metallic oxide, due to its higher conductivity and presence of several oxide states to facilitate a rich medium for redox reactions, has been reported [47]. The NiCo2O4 is fabricated as nanoneedles, directly grown on the conductive substrate, prepared by immersion in different solutions of cobalt nitrate, nickel nitrate, water, and ethanol using suitable ratios, heated at 85 °C after 8 h, annealed for 90 min at 250 °C have displayed specific capacitance of 118.6 F g−1 with retention of 89.4% after 2000 charging and discharging cycles [48].

Contrary to solid one-dimensional nanostructures, the nanotube structures offer a relatively high surface area with less utilization of electrode mass to deliver higher gravimetric capacitance. The nanotube structure fabrication can be attained by following the hydrothermal method, chemical vapour deposition, or by template-assisted methods is the most common method, wherein nanotubes are fabricated with ease thereby employing partial infiltration of nanotube forming substrate before deposition on the porous nanotubular template. In a template-assisted method, the substrate is partially anchored to the nanochannel walls during the early infiltration process. Thus, manipulating the infiltration process becomes essential to ensure the production of nanotubular structure. In this context, nanotubular MnO2 arrays have been reported as hollow one-dimensional electrodes for superconductors. The deposition of metallic material on an inactive supercapacitive material following a one-dimensional design provides an attempt to fabricate new supercapacitor devices. In this regard, AuPd@MnO2 core-shell structure has been reported [49]. The synthesis involved sputtering of AuPd on polyacrylonitrile nanopillars using spin-on-nanoimprinting method facilitated by electrochemical deposition of MnO2 is shown in Fig. 4 (a). The morphological images of the nanostructure are shown in Fig. 4 (b and c), revealing highly ordered nanopillar structure (200 nm-diameter, 250 nm-height and 200 nm-centre to centre distance). The nanostructure displayed a maximum specific capacitance of 603 F g−1.

Like zero-dimensional homostructure and heterostructures, one-dimensional nanostructures from the supercapacitor designing perspective are also grouped as homostructures and heterostructures. The one-dimensional structures discussed so far fall under the category of homostructures. The heterostructures following one-dimensional design are core-shell structures and one-dimensional composite nanostructures. The main advantage of using a one-dimensional heterostructures design is the synergistic improvement in the material's intrinsic properties for supercapacitor electrodes. The one-dimensional nanostructures designated for supercapacitor fabrications display overwhelming properties like better electrical conductivity, quicker transport, better electrochemical activity and reversibility, exceptional cyclic stability, and mechanical properties. Owing to these characteristics one-dimensional heterostructures are considered one of the most promising and potential candidates for the development of futuristic supercapacitor devices.

The core-shell one-dimensional heterostructure in this regard are generally nanorods, nanotubes, or nanowires coated on thin shells. The core-shell heterostructures are the most common design perspective considering the one-dimensional design due to unique structural properties that reduce surface energy. The reduction in surface energy effectively causes a reduction in the aggregation of active electrode materials, easing the reaction kinetics between the electrode material and the electrolyte. As a consequence of that, better reversibility and cyclic stability are achieved. Thus, the core-shell heterostructure one-dimensional nanostructure offers a possible designing approach when the rational synthesis approach is employed. Generally, one-dimensional core-shell nanostructures are prepared by one-dimensional backbone creation in the initial step, followed by the deposition of shell material via sheating. In this context, hydrothermal, electrospinning, chemical vapour deposition, sputtering and electrochemical deposition methods are commonly employed to design core-shell one-dimensional nanostructures. The core-shell design offers versatility in combining the supercapacitor electrode material in terms of current collector core-active material shell and active material formulating both core and shell in the design.

The one-dimensional core-shell designing approach using a core based on a current collector and a shell based on active material enables abbreviated pathways for the transport of electrons, thereby causing a significant enhancement in electrochemical activity. Metals' overwhelming mechanical and electrical conductivity makes them most suitable for this type of structural integrity. The metals selected for such configuration are forged as one-dimensional nanostructures in the form of nanowires or nanotubes, wherein active materials are directly deposited on the metallic frames to form core-shell structures. To design such structures, self-assembling, vapour phase deposition, template-assisted methods, soft solution processing methods, etc. are employed [50–53]. Among the mentioned methods, the template-assisted methods are extensively, widely explored, and employed for designing core-shell nanostructures. In particular, the one-dimensional metal-based nanostructures following the core-shell approach are fabricated using a sacrificial template or by the direct coating of active supercapacitor material on inactive one-dimensional nanostructures. In this context, a copper nanowire array fabricated by anode aluminum oxide template acting as a current collector followed by deposition of active material based on Fe3O4 has been reported [54]. A similar approach has been applied in the fabrication of supercapacitors based on Au@MnO2, FeMoO4@MnO2, ZnWO4@NiO, etc. [55–57]. The synthesis of AuPd@MnO2 following the core-shell approach for supercapacitors has demonstrated overwhelming electrochemical properties [58]. The core-shell structure included AuPd sputtering on polyaniline nanopillars using the spin-on nanoimprinting technique. The core-shell nanostructure demonstrated a highly ordered structure with a nanopillar diameter of (nearly) 140 nm with a height of (nearly) 250 nm, along with a center-center separation of (nearly) 200 nm. The AuPd@MnO2 core-shell nanostructure had a conformal coating of MnO2 on each nanopillar, thereby demonstrating maximum specific capacitance of 603 F g−1 with exceptional cyclic stability.

A one-dimensional nanostructure comprising a combination of two metal oxides viz; CuO and Co3O4 fabricated by electrospinning with nanowire morphology is shown in Fig. 5 (a and b) [52] has been reported recently. The combination provided one material with higher electrical conductivity and the other with higher charge stability, revealed by their structure and crystallinity, as depicted in Fig. 5 (c-f). The one-dimensional nanocomposite composed of CuO–Co3O4 delivered higher values for both specific capacitance and rate capability along with elevated cyclic stability than its components, as shown in Fig. 5 (g-j). The supercapacitor displayed higher energy ∼44 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 14 kW kg−1 in KOH electrolyte.

The other approach employed in the fabrication of one-dimensional nanostructure for supercapacitor application involves both core and shell composed of active material. The approach is also auspicious for yielding one-dimensional heterostructures wherein both the core and shell of the structure contribute to the overall electrochemical performance.

Generally, supercapacitors ate classified into two main types based on the active materials used; EDLC and pseudocapacitors. Therefore for active-core and active shell configurations, four types of supercapacitors can be portrayed viz.
•
EDLC core and EDLC shell combination
•
EDLC core and pseudocapacitor shell.
•
Pseudocapacitor shell and EDLC core.
•
Pseudocapacitor core and pseudocapacitor shell.

EDLC core material with pseudocapacitive material forming the shell formulates one of the most promising designs, thereby providing advantages like.
➢
The excellent cyclic stability provided by EDLC core as robust backbone without involving phase changes for many cycles.
➢
The EDLC core material provides better electrical conductivity in the design to facilitate better electron transport, along with features of the pseudocapacitive shell.
➢
Pseudocapacitive shell material effectively offsets the low capacitance of EDLC core material.

In this context, the feasible EDLC core material coated with pseudocapacitive shell material composed of carbon nanotubes@PPy-MnO2, carbon nanotubes@PANI-MnO2, and carbon nanotubes@PEDOT-MnO2 have been reported [59–61]. The core-shell structure is composed of carbon nanotubes@PPy-MnO2; firstly, multi-walled carbon nanotubes were dissolved, followed by wrapping 18 wt% poly (4-styrene sulfonic acid) to ease the bonding between pyrrole and metallic ions while undergoing discharging. The shell in the structure was prepared by adding pyrrole and KMnO4 for coating on carbon nanotubes. The core-shell structure demonstrated an overwhelming specific capacitance of (nearly) 268 F g−1 compared to similar core-shell structures [59]. The core-shell structure also demonstrated a rate capability of 93%.

The pseudocapacitive core and pseudocapacitive shell combination also formulate an intriguing designing perspective in the fabrication of one-dimensional supercapacitors. This designing perspective offers the advantage of redox reactions in core and shell material while undergoing the charging and discharging process, resulting in higher values of specific capacitance. In this context, one-dimensional core-shell supercapacitors like Co3O4@MnO2, Co3O4@NiO, CoO@TiO2, and PEDOT-MnO2 are reported [62–65]. The core-shell structure formulated using Co3O4@MnO2 as nanowire using the hydrothermal method displayed a specific capacitance of 480 F g−1 and excellent rate capability. The most common one-dimensional materials reported for supercapacitor applications are portrayed in Table 2 .

The significance of diffusion on length, contact area, utilization of electrode, and active material on the electrochemical performance of the supercapacitors has led to the rise of two-dimensional nanostructures designs for supercapacitors. The two-dimensional nanostructures have advantages like effective surface area without incorporating bulk volume and the versatility of different possible configurations to develop hybrid two-dimensional structures. Additionally, two-dimensional nanostructures exhibit exceptional stability in terms of mechanical strength, flexibility, chemical structure, and transparency. Considering two-dimensional nanostructures for supercapacitors, limited information is available in terms of their unique behaviour and mostly two-dimensional nanostructures for supercapacitors are reported using aqueous electrolytes or organic solvents. The two-dimensional materials are believed to envisage a paradigm shift in terms of electrochemical parameters [66].

Like one-dimensional nanostructures, two-dimensional nanostructures also engulf two-dimensional homo and two-dimensional heterostructures. The two-dimensional homostructures developed for supercapacitors include graphene-based electrodes, transition metal oxide/hydroxide-based electrodes and transition metal dichalcogenides. Among the three, graphene and metal oxide/hydroxide-based supercapacitors have been extensively studied and commercialized. The transition metal dichalcogenides, including MOS2 and VS2, along with transition metal carbides/nitrites, have been a recent addition to this category. The emergence of graphene has provided a more suitable and versatile alternative to carbon-based supercapacitor electrodes with relatively better electrochemical properties. Graphene as supercapacitor electrodes overshadows the limitations observed in carbon-based supercapacitors. The graphene-based electrodes also allow different optimization for better material utilization as a supercapacitor. As such, currently, graphene is among the most common materials devoted to the development of supercapacitors due to its higher chemical stability in two-dimensional structures, and unique electrical, thermal and mechanical properties [67–69]. Graphene in monolayer morphology tenders areal and average specific capacitance in the range of 21 μF cm−1 and 50 F g−1, respectively, as a double-layer capacitor [70]. The higher value is attributed to the surface optimizations, enabling full utilization of active material. However, the designing of graphene-based EDLC's remains a challenging perspective.

The structural designs for different types of two-dimensional supercapacitors are depicted in Fig. 6 to allow fabrication of flexible supercapacitors [68]. The two-dimensional nanostructures fabricated following a flexible design approach have already shown great promise due to exceptional mechanical and synergistic effects, yielding better electrochemical performance.

The use of graphene as supercapacitor electrodes does not require the incorporation of any conductive filler to enhance conductivity [71]. The higher surface area of graphene in two-dimensional form (nearly) 2630 m2g-1 also attracts much attention for its consideration as a supercapacitor electrode. The graphene structure consists of many accessible pores along with wider inner spacing between the planes. The layered graphene two-dimensional homostructures can be prepared by inkjet printing, chemical vapour deposition, electrostatic spray deposition, etc [72–74].

Among transition metal oxides/hydroxides RuO2, IrO2, MnO2, NiO, Co2O3, SnO2, V2O5 and MoO2 formulate an important class of candidates for two-dimensional nanostructures for supercapacitor. The development of hybrid devices mainly incorporates two-dimensional transition metal oxides/hydroxides as the central material. The development of two-dimensional hybrid structures using transition metal oxides/hydroxides mainly involves techniques like atomic layer deposition, spin coating, sputtering, anodization, electrodeposition, and spray deposition. By employing these methods, smooth thin films with texture optimizations along with low grain boundary densities [75]. The two-dimensional MnO2 has been one of the most prominent choices for the supercapacitor cathode. Using the soft template method, specific capacitance values of up to 775 F g−1 have been attained while undergoing 10,000 charging and discharging cycles [76]. The soft template method prepared electrodes were immersed in an aqueous gel electrolyte (Ca(NO3)2–SiO2). The two-dimensional nanostructures using transition metal oxides/hydroxides have delivered specific capacitance in the range of 500–1000 F g−1 [77].

Layered transition metal dichalcogenides in their monolayer form have recently emerged as an essential and promising candidate in the field of material science for different applications. The applicability of these materials has been prominent in optoelectronics, nanoelectronics, sensors, and electrochemical storage. These materials offer some of the most promising properties that overshadow the limitations of graphene. However, currently, their exploration of supercapacitors stays restricted or limited. Among these materials, MoS2 has attracted significant interest in supercapacitors due to their exceptional conductivity, which is relatively higher than other transition metal oxides [78]. The electrode material prepared using MoS2 displayed specific conductance of 168 F g−1 while retaining 93% after 3000 cycles [79]. MXenes have been among the most trending materials, also known as transition metal carbides/nitrides. They also fall under the category of two-dimensional homostructures. Their introduction to supercapacitors has been a very recent addition to the materials designed for electrochemical storage. MXenes are highly conductive carbides/carbonitrides layers with surface functionalities. The use of MXenes, as per reports, has yielded a specific capacitance of >300 F cm−3 [80]. The MXenes Ti3C2Tz prepared by etching Ti3AlC2 using HCl at room temperature has been reported as two-dimensional supercapacitors [81]. The binder-restrained MXene exhibited a surface area of 98 m2 g−1 while undergoing delamination, forming a single to few layers, which under filtration, resulted in Ti3AlC2. The MXene exhibited high flexibility with greater values for volumetric capacitance in an aqueous electrolyte. The double-layer capacitor based on Ti3C2 MXene using two-dimensional flexible design combined features of excellent electrical conductivity alongside hydrophilic surface to demonstrate electrochemical performance attributed to the intercalation is shown in Fig. 7 [74]. The flexible two-dimensional MXene-based supercapacitor in KOH electrolyte demonstrated excellent areal specific capacitance of 340 F cm−1, slightly better than graphene. The approach of applying MXene not only displays exceptional flexibility along with good electrical conductivity paves a new way to develop high-performance supercapacitors using an exclusive feature of intercalation.

The two-dimensional heterostructure dedicated to supercapacitors is mostly a combination of graphene with transition metal oxides, dichalcogenides, and a few hybrid configurations. The graphene metal oxide/hydroxide as supercapacitor electrodes offers great potential in developing EDLCs. The combination tends to be a unique blend of properties to ensure chemically stable [82]. The combination offers higher stability over longer cycles, controlled crystallinity, and better rate performance [83].

The graphene dichalcogenides, although being a new addition, has been very trending since their exploration as supercapacitor electrodes based on two-dimensional nanostructures. Reports have made few attempts to incorporate metal sulphides with graphene as a mixture for supercapacitor electrodes. The mixture type of electrode for supercapacitor exhibits good electrochemically activity, therefore portraying their potential for energy storage devices [84]. Hence, much effort is required to explore their full potential. However, in this context, MoS2 and reduced graphene oxide has been reported for hybrid energy storage [85]. The presence of covalent bonds between MOS2 and the oxygen atom in reduced graphene oxide results in higher energy density (63 Wh Kg−1) with exceptional stability. The various two two-dimensional nanostructures are enlisted in Table 3 .

The three-dimensional nanostructure is the latest and most well-defined design approach in developing next-generation supercapacitors. The three-dimensional porous nanostructures exhibit large surface area and mechanical stability and provide efficient and well-defined pathways for the electrolyte accessibility for supercapacitor electrodes. The processing of three-dimensional electrodes involves the use of metallic foam as the template for the arrangement of active electrode materials into three-dimensional nanostructures. Apart from metallic foam as a template, the active electrode materials are also directly deposited on the three-dimensional nanostructure.

The three-dimensional nanostructure designs are extended viz.
•
Metallic foam as current collector in three-dimensional nanostructures.
•
Carbon-based three-dimensional nanostructures.
•
Pseudocapacitive material-based three-dimensional structures

In the first type of three-dimensional structure design, nickel foam is the most common example. It is used as a current collector, with highly porous and conductive material deposited directly on it to produce supercapacitor electrodes. The active materials directly deposited on nickel foam include carbonaceous materials, polymers, and metal oxides/hydroxides for supercapacitor electrodes [86]. In contrast to carbon-based substrates, metallic foam exhibits higher electrical conductivity and better mechanical strength with more significant density and lower specific surface area. The three-dimensional Co and Ni oxides with different structural morphology under a controlled synthesis environment have been reported using Ni foam for their deposition [87]. The preparation of Ni/Co-based electrodes deposited on Ni foam to fabricate three-dimensional nanostructures involves co-electro deposition of Ni and Co bimetallic hydroxide precursors into Ni foam scaffold followed by simultaneous thermal transformation of the precursors into subsequent oxides. These materials' structural, morphological and electrochemical properties can easily be optimized by varying the molar ratio of Co/Ni with structural dominance shown by NiCo2O over NiO crystal while finally changing to Co3O4 dominant structure. The unique and exciting architecture formed by the Co/Ni oxide network provides a higher surface area in terms of solid/liquid interface to enable facile and efficient transport of electrons and ions. The electrodes prepared from Ni/Co oxide on nickel foam exhibit a specific capacitance of 1523 F g−1 at a current density of 2 A g−1 with capacity retention of 95.30% after 1000 cycles. In electrochemical density, the Ni/Co oxide electrodes served as positive electrodes. At the same time, activated carbon was used as a negative electrode.

The second class of three-dimensional nanostructures is based on carbonaceous materials for supercapacitive devices. In this context, mesoporous carbon has been one of the most promising materials due to its large surface area, tunability and interconnected pores. The mesoporous carbon has been fabricated from the ordered mesoporous silica with molecular sieves serving as templates [88]. In the last few decades, mesoporous carbon-based supercapacitors have been one of the most prominently explored materials with much research, dedication, and devotion. The studies on mesoporous carbon serving as electrode material for EDLC portray the effects of pore distribution, pore size and symmetry on the performance based on electrolyte accessibility [89]. The mesoporous carbon-based EDLCs have reported a maximum specific capacitance of 140 F g−1, which is relatively higher than regular carbon.

The three-dimensional structure formulated kenaf stem-based porous carbon using MnO2 has shown great potential as a low-cost material for energy storage by electrochemical means [89]. The morphology of the nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 8 . The morphology of the nanocomposite depicts an ordered macroporous structure due to the carbonization of kenaf stems. The nanocomposite was prepared by electrodeposition to incorporate flower-like MnO2 nanosphere uniformly is shown in Fig. 8 (b and c). The morphology also revealed holes between the flower-like MnO2 enabling efficient diffusion of ions, thereby providing efficient pathways. The nanocomposite contained adhesion of MnO2 both externally and internally along the surface and within the structure, respectively, is shown in Fig. 8 (d-f) under high resolution.

When subjected to electrochemical testing using three electrode setups, the three-dimensional nanocomposite showed promising results. The cyclic voltammetry results for the three-dimensional nanocomposite are depicted in Fig. 9 (a-d). The nanocomposite displayed dominant double layer behavior, as confirmed by the shape of voltammograms.

For carbonaceous materials, the specific capacitance values can further be enhanced by doping or deposition of pseudocapacitive materials like conducting polymers and metal oxides to formulate hybrid configurations. In this context, Nitrogen and sulfur-doped mesoporous carbon has been reported to have a specific capacitance of 320 F g−1 [90]. The incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur into mesoporous carbon serves as a pseudocapacitive medium thereby enhancing overall specific capacitance.

Finally, the three-dimensional nanostructures based on pseudocapacitors provide an intriguing composition for next-generation supercapacitors. Following the configuration, different types of metal oxides in nanostructures have been reported, prepared by solvothermal, sol-gel, and other approaches. NiO and MnO2 have been the most prominent candidates in the category. NiO is reported in particle, slice, and flower morphologies, following the sol-gel method [91].

NiO in the three morphologies reveals specific surface area values of 233, 193, and 159 m2 g−1, respectively. The lowest surface area exhibited by nanoflower NiO demonstrated the largest pore sizes to facilitate efficient contact and transport of electrolyte ions within the three-dimensional structure. MnO2, in this regard, has been reported as an urchin-like nanostructure prepared by the hydrothermal method and clew-like structures [92]. The phase products of MnO2 transformed from alpha-MnO2-Epsilon-MnO2 while replacing Al3+ with Fe3+ in the solution. The phase transformation leads to a change in structure from an urchin to a three-dimensional claw structure. The maximum specific capacitance attained from MnO2 three-dimensional nanostructure was 120 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1.

The designing perspective elaborated in section two can be extended to fabricate green supercapacitors. The extension, however, requires a lot of devotion and research exploration, with limited green material options for supercapacitors. Following these designing perspectives green, smart, flexible and wearable supercapacitors can be fabricated to serve as next-generation energy-storing devices, using the electrochemical conversion principle for energy storage. The various materials dedicated for supercapacitor applications following three-dimensional nanostructures are enliseted in Table 4 .

Currently, the components of electronic devices, like the energy storing units, suffer from the disadvantages of a constrained lifetime. However, the continuous exploration of novel and fresh electrical energy storage devices counters the problem of the exponential rise in the mass of used in electrochemical devices like batteries and supercapacitors, which requires substantial processing to reduce negative environmental impact. Annually, more than 10 billion batteries are discarded globally, with a projection of battery waste per person greater than 130 g per year [93].

In addition to the electrochemical device wastes, the disposal of waste from mobile phones, computers, gaming consoles, and other autonomous electrical devices is also rising exponentially, thereby imposing disposal concerns related to the environmental impact. In this regard, supercapacitors, to some extent, have reduced the concerns related to the lifetime of energy-storing devices compared to their counterparts. However, their integration with the devices marks their disposal at the time of device expiry, thereby restricting their fullest applicability. The disposal of electrochemical energy devices causes serious threats to the environment due to the presence of toxic metals like Pb, Ni, Cd, F-containing electrolytes, and other device components. If unregulated, the toxic nature of these components can cause serious environmental threats, thereby inducing severe impacts on human health due to their continuous exposure of toxic products for a longer time. The negative impact on the environment is also due to inefficient, expensive processing involved in the disposal of toxic components. In addition, the lack of preference towards the disposal of these components over general waste also reduces attention towards their disposal. The disposal issues are not constrained to battery waste only, it can be extended to the materials used to develop supercapacitors, thereby imposing the same concerns. The electrolytes containing F-based salts like tetraethylammarium detraflourosorate, binder particles like polyvinyldiflouride, polytetrafluoroethylene, carbon particles and separator materials are also likely to generate volatile fluorocarbons while conventional incineration. The compounds are highly carcinogenic to organisms with the portrayal of incinerator danger and nearby structural damage. The supercapacitor electrolytes, like acetonitrile solvents, used to improve electrochemical performance are highly flammable, toxic, carcinogenic, and decompose into highly toxic cyanides under heating [94]. Also, ionic liquid electrolytes like bis((trifluoromethanesulfonyl) imide (TFSI)) produce a negative impact on cellular respiration [95].

Contrary to that, using aqueous electrolytes causes a negligible environmental impact. However, the aqueous electrolytes emit SO2 contributing to acid rain on release into the environment while incineration [96]. Compared to batteries and fuel cells, supercapacitors are free from heavy or noble metals, which are difficult to dispose of. The current collectors and sealing materials in supercapacitors contain elements like aluminum and steel, which are incombustible and do not leave any ash residue. Thus, the routine disposal of supercapacitor components leads to the generation of toxic and harmful substances in addition to the incombustible materials to be stored in a landfill.

The conventional supercapacitor components and their proposed green material alternatives are portrayed in Fig. 10 . The alternative for electrode materials based on carbon and their allotropes can be synthesized with optimized microporosity and a larger specific area (>1500 m2 g−1) from corn-biomass, seaweed, and coconut shells with minimal environmental impact [97–100]. In addition, alternatives in cellulose and polystyrene have been developed for conducting polymer electrode materials to replace fluorine-based polymer as binders. However, cellulose and biomaterials do not support free-standing film structures, thereby restricting high charge and discharge performance [101]. The electrolytes used in the supercapacitors mainly on conductive (SO4)−2 salts, acids, or fluorine-based salts dissolved in acetonitrile or propylene carbonate [102]. Apart from electrodes and electrolytes, the current collectors for supercapacitors, are conventionally made of steel, aluminum, or nickel which are non-combustible. The current collectors for batteries are recently made from combustible options like carbon foam and graphite sheets etc. [103,104]. However, this approach is yet to be applied to supercapacitors.

Recently a thick carbon-based electrode comprising of ultrahigh mass loading ∼40 mg cm−1 using activation and carbonization of basswood as green supercapacitor has been reported [104]. The green supercapacitor was designed to function under extreme conditions of quick charging/discharging to exhibit ≥50000 cycles at an extremely low temperature of −40 °C. The carbon-based green supercapacitor inherited vertical channels of basswood enabling efficient mass transport of electrolytic ions along with enhanced ionic penetration. The morphology of the green supercapacitor owes rational micro/meso-sized pores and oxygen-containing functional groups stimulated by H2O2 activation to enhance ion transport kinetics. In terms of electrochemical performance, the supercapacitor delivers specific capacitance of 6205.7 mF cm−2 (221.6 F g−1 and 77.6 F cm−3) and 4886.4 mF cm−2 (174.5 F g−1, 61.1 F cm−3) at ambient temperature and −40 °C, respectively. Additionally, the supercapacitor demonstrated retention of 90.6% after 70000 cycles thereby portraying its candidature as promising supercapacitor working at extremely low temperatures.

The excessive usage of fossil fuels, rising energy demand and growing concerns related to the environmental pollution had opened a new window for the creation and development of pioneering, environment friendly, high performance and renewable energy storing systems. A green supercapacitor based on the conversion of biomass derived waste into cost-effective and high-performance supercapacitor has been reported, using banana peel as a heteroatom-doped carbon (H-PC) precursor to develop high energy density, quicker charging, and superior long-term supercapacitor electrodes based on large-scale synthesis [105]. The synthesis involved carbonization of dried banana peel at 800 °C for 2 h under the argon atmosphere was homogeneously mixed 20 wt% of MoS2 by the dry-impregnation method followed by layer-by layer deposition of MoS2-decorated H-PC (MoS2@H-PC) composite owning micro/mesoporous structure, and satisfactory surface area (210 m2 g−1). The prepared MoS2@H-PC nanocomposite exhibited considerable faradaic reactions and electrostatic adsorption due to the presence of abundant electrochemical active sites leading to a profound specific capacitance of 408 F g−1 at a current density of 1 A g−1. Furthermore, MoS2@H-PC reveals impressive cyclic stability with 90% capacitance retention beyond 10,000 cycles thereby providing a suitable approach for the development of high energy density and robust supercapacitors from various agricultural waste products and landfills.

A green synthesis route was followed for the fabrication of low-cost of lotus carbon (LC) materials using lotus parts including leaves, flowers, fruits (seed pods), and stems as a renewable precursor as shown in Fig. 11 . The synthesis involved carbonization of different porous carbons, leaf-carbon (LF-carbon), flower-carbon (FL-carbon), fruit-carbon (FR-carbon), and stem-carbon (ST-carbon) from different parts of the lotus plant by simple carbonization method [106]. The mesoporous structure of LC materials recommended its applicability as supercapacitor. The obtained FR-Carbon exhibits a high specific capacitance of 160 F g−1 in a three-electrode system in an aqueous 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte with a high rate performance of 52% retention from 0.5 to 5.0 A/g with good cycling stability of 95%. These results indicate that the porous carbon derived from lotus fruits is a potential electrode material for high-performance supercapacitors.

A exclusive heteroatom-doped spongy carbonaceous material from dwarf banana peel synthesized using the one-step hydrothermal method has been reported for green supercapacitors [107] The synthesis involved use of redundant banana peel as a carbon source for the formation of heteroatom-doped porous carbon. This biowaste-derived heteroatom-doped porous carbonaceous material (BH-PCM) exhibited interrelated pores with surface area of 213 m2 g−1. While examining the fabricated electrode material for supercapacitor using a three-electrode system in aqueous 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte solution exhibited excellent specific capacitance of 137 F g−1 at 0.5 A g−1 at an impressive rate performance with a capacitance enduring 51 F g−1 at 5.0 A g−1. After 10,000 galvanostatic charge–discharge cycles, the electrodes retained 94% of its initial capacitance. The fabricated symmetrical two-electrode cell displayed gravimetric specific capacitances of 87 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The excellent electrochemical performance of the electrodes is attributed to their high surface area, reasonable heteroatom doping rate, and a suitable degree of graphitization. This study offers a green approach for the development of environmental-friendly potential carbon-based electrode, by converting biowaste to clean/green energy.

The solid waste dumping and exhausting energy resources has raised global concerns thereby affecting the world's ecology and economy through environmental pollution and fuel crisis. A solid waste-derived carbon/metal oxide composite electrode for supercapacitors has been reported in this regard using purpose, an infant-urinated waste diapers from the major municipality waste as a nitrogen-doped carbon source to develop a facile and cost-effective electrode material [108]. The presence of urea/uric acid in the urinated diaper can contribute nitrogen atoms to carbon suitable for enhancing the electrical conductivity of the carbon electrode. NiO act as pseudocapacitor material for compensating the shortage of volumetric and gravimetric performance in carbon. The fabricated solid waste-derived carbon fibers-trapped nickel oxide composite electrode exhibited a specific capacitance of 356 F g−1 at a discharge current of 2 A g−1 with robust cycle stability after 5000 cycles with a current density of 10 A g−1. This study offers a green approach for the development of environmentally favorable potential carbon electrodes, by converting solid waste to clean energy.

The electrode materials based on Ni- and Co-oxide materials have been most promising options for electrocatalytic properties due to the oxygen evolution reaction (OER) and the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), in addition of being low cost, abundance and environmental friendliness. The stability of these materials in alkaline media marks their strong candidature for applications such as a gas diffusion electrode (GDE) for rechargeable metal-air batteries. In this regard a novel concept using carbon-free gas GDE design has been reported as supercapacitors [109]. The synthesis involved hot pressing of mixture of catalyst (Co3O4, NiCo2O4) and polytetrafluoroethylene onto a stainless-steel mesh as the current collector. To enhance the electrical conductivity 70 wt% Ni powder was included. The GDEs produced in this way were examined in a half-cell configuration with a 6 M KOH electrolyte, stainless steel counter electrode, and hydrogen reference electrode at room temperature. The electrochemical behavior of the gas-diffusion electrodes; Ni/Co3O4 and Ni/NiCo2O4, displayed acceptable over potential for OER and ORR along with better mechanical and chemical stability of electrodes consisting of Ni/NiCo2O4 (70:30 wt%).
This review section highlights the different materials or environment-friendly alternatives for supercapacitor components to fabricate green supercapacitors.

The metallic ones, as current collectors, exhibit the property of non-flammability and hence are difficult to dispose of. In this context, alternatives to metallic current collectors were provided by using carbon-based materials with features like higher conductivity and better interfacial adhesion with electrode materials. The other properties required for developing eco-friendly or green material-based current collectors include low cross-sectional thickness and flexibility to reduce overall device mass. Based on these features, three types of carbonaceous materials were applied and tested as current collectors for supercapacitors using sandwich-type cell configurations. Among the three, the first one is composed of carbon nanotubes aligned as bundles in paper structures to exhibit higher conductivity. The second one included carbon-fiber-based mats interwoven with graphitized fibers exhibiting better adhesion, thereby providing conductive bridges with electrode materials. Finally, the third type of carbon used as a current collector is composed of graphite in the form of foil stocked as sheets exhibiting exceptional flexibility.

The carbonaceous material used as the current collector for supercapacitors, viz carbon nanotube in the form of paper or the carbon nanofiber mats, does not render sufficient conductivity, although derived from graphite units. The limited conductivity of these materials was determined from four-probe measurements (0.07–0.09 S cm−1). In addition to that form impedance spectroscopy, the materials displayed equivalent series resistance greater than 200 Ω, thereby restraining specific capacitance values. Considering the listed aspects, these materials with intensive and exotic fabrication processing for their exploration and better implementation as green current collectors have suffered to a greater extent. Contrary to that, the current collector material derived from stacked graphene sheets improved the gravimetric performance of supercapacitors. The electrochemical analysis of the graphite-based current collectors in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte is depicted in Fig. 12 . The specific capacitance was less than metallic current collectors by 26% using the same configuration [103]. Thus among the three dedicated materials, only graphite-based materials showed some promising results as green material current collectors for supercapacitors.

The main objective behind developing green supercapacitors requires the development of green electrolyte materials without F and S-containing compounds, which mainly dispose of toxic substances. Additionally, the electrolytes using acetonitrile on decomposition produce cyanides. Thus, developing green electrolytes requires maximum component safety, affordability, and efficient disposal. The toxic and corrosive nature of the commonly used supercapacitor aqueous electrolyte using H2SO4 and KOH makes them non-ideal for green supercapacitor designs. On the other hand, sodium acetate has been reported along with Sodium formate for S-free electrolyte alternatives. These S-free alternatives render reduced diameters of desolvated anions [104]. The ions produced by these electrolytes on complete salvation fit in most electrode material pores [110]. The electrochemical analysis for these electrolytes is depicted in Fig. 13 with specific capacitance values of 99 F g−1 for sodium acetate and 81 F g−1 for Na2SO4 typical electrolyte. The sodium acetate electrolyte exhibited higher rate capability with a similar frequency response as that of Na2SO4, thereby portraying stable ion dynamics, swift mobility, similar resistance and diffusion parameters. However, the ion mobility in these electrolytes strongly depends on the solution concentration.

Considering the green alternatives to aqueous electrolytes, 1-sutyl-3-methyl-imidazoium acetate ionic liquid has been considered and tested in an inert (Ar) environment. However, this ionic liquid electrolyte yielded a specific capacitance of 84 F g−1 at a low scan rate, with poor rate capability and higher resistance values. The ionic liquid at room temperature exhibits constrained mobility when used without solvent. However, it exhibits similar behavior at higher temperatures as organic solvent electrolytes [111].

The separator is one of the critical components in supercapacitor structures, playing an essential role in the transportation of ions or ion mobility from one component to another. The primary requirement for developing green supercapacitors using green material-based separators like electrolytes is the absence of F and S elements. Thus the separators developed for green supercapacitors must exhibit electrochemically stable compounds. In this context, porous membranes have been developed and implemented to fabricate green supercapacitors, currently used in chemical filtration. Such materials are readily available and offer low-cost features. The criteria for selecting these materials are electrochemical stability, ionic mobility, and environmental impact. The features of porous membranes enable their integration with other suitable low environmental impact materials to formulate a suitable material to deliver optimum performance and offer safe disposal. These materials' structural integrity exhibited various porous structures within their morphology. Apart from this, the morphological analysis revealed the macroscopic thickness of these porous membranes [112]. The coupling of these porous membranes with functional graphs inhibits features like wet ability and enhancement in the electrochemical performance of the separator. The different porous membranes employed as supercapacitor separators are depicted in Fig. 14 . These porous membrane separator morphologies are classified as track etched containing straight and singular pores, along with interwoven textile structures. The specific surface area for these porous membrane materials ranges between 0.5 and 4.9 m2 g−1 and includes materials like paper, cellulose acetate, etc.

The supercapacitors using separators based on acetate, cellulose, nylon and polyethersulfonate have shown promising results in electrochemical performance and environmental impact [113]; among these, cellulose esters containing terminated ester graphs tender best rate capability and environmental efficiency as supercapacitor separators.

Electrodes are the main components of the supercapacitors, based on which maximum electrochemical properties of the device can be portrayed. In context to this development of green supercapacitors, the electrode materials for such devices must exhibit features like low toxicity, safe disposal, and better electrochemical activity. Electronic waste management has already rung alarming bells globally, resulting in a large quantity of landfill waste and pollution in water bodies [114]. Most countries have started comprehensive programs for electronic waste management [115]. However, the challenges in electronic waste management include high-cost techniques and toxicity levels. Since electrochemical capacitors or supercapacitors are already portrayed as attractive and clean energy systems. These features mainly arise from the properties of the electrode materials employed for the fabrication of supercapacitors. Considering, green supercapacitors, numerous material options are considered for developing eco-friendly supercapacitors like activated carbon; exhibiting high surface area, porosity, and high electrical conductivity. The activated carbon derived from paper waste has shown good electrochemical properties [116]. The activated carbon derived from waste tissue paper and hardboards has also been reported as green electrode material for supercapacitors [117]. The paper and hardboard bits were treated with diluted H2SO4 and transferred to Teflon autoclave following a hydrothermal procedure for 20 h at optimized temperatures of 180 °C. The product was washed and filtered after reaction kinetics using a hot air oven (70 °C) overnight for drying. The powder attained was grinded and activated using KOH at room temperature, followed by acid-based purification. The activated carbon procured by the hydrothermal method showed spherical morphology, exhibiting high surface area, is shown in Fig. 15 .

The electrochemical results of the activated carbon-based supercapacitor electrodes are shown in Fig. 16 . The maximum specific capacitance of activated carbon-based electrodes was 260 F g−1 at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 with retention of 92%.

Following a similar fabrication approach, activated carbon derived from cassava peels treated by KOH and H3PO4 has been reported [118]. The electrochemical performance of the electrodes using three electrodes set up revealed the highest capacitance of 64.18 F g−1 with an effective surface area of 398.46 m2 g−1.

In another report, carbonized bamboo fibers were prepared for operation as energy storage over a wide temperature range [119]. The bamboo fibers were portrayed as flexible supercapacitors and carbonized following a two-step procedure. The first step comprised of hydrothermal procedure, wherein 1 g of bamboo fibers were dispersed in 30 ml H2SO4 in a Teflon container for reaction kinetics at an optimized temperature of 180 °C for 24 h. After the first step, the fibers were washed and filtered several times using DI water, followed by overnight drying at 70 °C. The penetrated fibers were then activated chemically using KOH in a ratio of 1:1. The activated mixture was then finely grinded and heated at 800 °C for 1 h under an ambient Ar environment followed by cooling at room temperature and washed with HCL and DI water. The final product was dried using an oven at an optimum 70 °C. The hydrothermally prepared bamboo fibers revealed three-dimensional, interconnected random morphology, thereby highlighting porous structure with low temperature. Furthermore, the carbonized bamboo using 3 M KOH as electrolyte delivered stable electrochemical activity as shown in Fig. 17 .

The cyclic voltammograms are rectangular in shape thereby showing no evidence of redox peaks, as shown in Fig. 18 . Therefore the electrodes show purely electrostatic double-layer behavior with a maximum specific capacitance of 415 F g−1.

Due to the overwhelming features of activated carbon and recent trends in the development of green supercapacitors, the production or derivation of activated carbon from bio-waste has provided a suitable alternative for the development of sustainable and green energy devices. The bio-waste sources for derivation of activated carbon include animal sources, minerals, plants, and vegetables, etc., to develop electrochemical devices [120–123]. The activated carbon derived from the sources has been tested as EDLC using organic electrolytes for feasibility.

In this context, coconut shells based on porous activated carbon have been reported to exhibit tunability regarding pore distribution, pore-ratio as supercapacitor [124]. The activated carbon derived from coconut shells has been procured by using one-step thermal treatment, pyrolysis, and steam activation to yield porous activated carbon. The electrochemical performance of the biowaste coconut shell-based electrodes is depicted in Fig. 19 . The cyclic voltammetry reveals a quasi-rectangular plot portraying exemplary double-layer capacitor behavior with a maximum capacitance of 228 F g−1 at the scan rate of 5 m V s−1. The specific capacitance of the electrodes stands proportional to the activation time and flow rate of water. The electrode material exhibited excellent conductivity as per the charge/discharge profile.

Following the bio-waste-based ideas, many researchers have explored their vision towards developing energy-storing material from agricultural crops and their residues. In this context, hydrothermal method was deployed to fabricate supercapacitor electrodes based on pre-treated sugarcane bagasse to yield a three-dimensional, high surface, interconnected & conducting carbon nanochannels [125].
