The procedure and morphological analysis of the hydrothermally prepared electrode material is shown in Fig. 20 . The morphological analysis reveals the presence of different forms of carbon. The fabricated electrodes were subjected to electrochemical analysis using three electrodes set up. The results revealed a specific capacitance of 200 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 with an energy density of 7 W h Kg−1 concerning a power density of 571 W kg−1.

Green electrode material in the form of activated carbon from soybean roots has been reported for supercapacitor [126]. The Carbonization of soybean roots was carried out at a temperature of 900 °C for 2 h under an ambient nitrogen environment. The prepared samples exhibited high porosity and by two electrode electrochemical testing set up, yielding a specific capacitance of 276 F g−1 at 5 A g−1 with retention of 98% after 1000 cycles. The electrochemical performance of the sample is depicted in Fig. 21 using an ionic liquid electrolyte. The specific energy density as per the estimates, was 100.5 W h Kg−1 concerning power density of 4353 W kg−1.
The other reported green material-based electrodes and electrochemical performance, are summarized in Table 5 .

In recent years, the research interest for supercapacitor development has shifted towards renewable, cheap, biodegradable, biocompatible cool and lignocellulose-based materials. The functionality of lignocellulose-based materials has been of great research interest in terms of energy storage & environmental pollution. To attain the desired characteristics supermolecular self-assembly and surface modification techniques have been employed to avail multi-functional materials [146]. For supercapacitor applications, in particular, the wood-based materials must exhibit characteristics like low cost, lightweight, flexibility and sustainability. In this context, the wood or lignocellulose-based materials are fabricated as the porous skeleton, flexible substrate-based, biocompatible and electroactive component-induced structures. Considering the porous skeleton structures, the assembly comprises of a well-organized arrangement of electrochemically active materials engulfing higher mass loading to facilitate ionic and electron transfer. In this regard, an anisotropic wood consisting of straight hierarchical channels exhibits a controlled assembly of capacitive nanoparticles on the cell walls using simple methods to yield high field high mass loading and high energy density for the procured supercapacitor electrodes [147]. The robust cellulose networks by engulfing conductive sheath enable higher accessible area for electrochemical activity thereby enabling better electrochemical performance.

Considering the flexible substrate capable of supporting electrochemically active materials endowing free-standing electrodes, the mechanical strength and flexibility are essential for portable, compact and foldable electronics. In this regard, a typical graphene-cellulose paper electrode material has been reported [148]. The assembly consisted of finely dispersed graphene nanosheets, intervened within the cellulose network following a simple filtration, printing or coating of slurry, The graphene cellulose network, due to the presence of cellulose paper provides good mechanical strength, structural stability and flexibility thereby enabling composite electrodes to store energy after undergoing repeated bending and folding without deviation to electrochemical performance [149,150]. Apart from that, the biocompatible cellulose products in contrast to polyester fibers, when employed as supercapacitor electrodes exhibit excellent wettability and permeability to aqueous electrolytes due to porous structure and functional group presence, hence suitable for wearable devices.

Wood has been successfully employed as a precursor in the forms of carbon like cellulose, and hemicellulose and lignin to develop activated carbon for supercapacitors. Wood/cellulose-derived carbon is found in different forms like carbon paper, carbon fibers, and carbon aerogels, which exhibit porosity to produce self-supporting electrode materials featuring high conductivity and surface area to deliver excellent electrochemical performance.

The carbon materials derived from bio-mass has been very popular in development of electrochemical devices. The various bio-mass forms uses to derive carbon for energy storage are displayed in Fig. 22 [151]. The bio-mass derived carbon upgrading by introduction of cellulosic residue also referred as hemp using carbonization technique is an efficient mode to develop cheap, sustainable and green energy storing devices. In a recent study a porous carbon derived from bio-mass precursor has been reported as a green supercapacitor [152]. The electrode material was derived from tea waste as cheap and sustainable energy-storing material for eco-friendly supercapacitors. The prepared carbonaceous material exhibited a large surface area of 1610 m2 g−1 with a specific capacitance of 332 F g−1 at 100 Ag-1 using 6 M KOH electrolyte. Apart from that, the carbonaceous electrodes exhibited excellent cyclic life after undergoing 100,000 charging/discharging cycles thereby losing only 2.2 % of its initial capacitance.

A green synthesis route was employed in the development of an expandable graphene sheet to platinum nanoparticles on an indium tin oxide glass electrode substrate [153]. The green synthesis procured nanocomposite with better catalytic activity, electrical conductivity along with good electrochemical stability. The prepared nanocomposite electrodes were subjected to repeated charging/discharging cycles to attain a steady state while attaining a forward current peaks density of 7.41 mA cm−2 at a respective voltage of 0.92 V. In particular, for supercapacitor applications, the amalgamation of conducting polymers and graphene to fabricate novel electrodes has recently been a popular trend. The combination of graphene with polyaniline has been reported in this regard. The green synthesis approach was employed to fabricate green supercapacitor electrodes, exhibiting electroactive regions for ionic diffusion [154,155]. The polyaniline graphene-based electrode displays a higher specific surface of 2600 m2 g−1 [156]. The nanocomposite electrodes also depict a higher specific capacitance of 210 F g−1 which is in a close contest with the ones synthesized following non-green synthesis routes [157].

A green Synthesis route was employed to fabricate supercapacitor electrodes using graphene quantum dots doped with polyaniline and polyacrylonitrile [158]. The nanocomposite displayed better conductivity (2.362 × 10−6 S m−1)with optical absorbance of λmax-270 nm. The nanocomposite exhibited better electrochemical properties using 0.1 M H2SO4, electrolyte. The nanocomposite reported a maximum specific areal capacitance of 587 F cm−1.

In another report a low-cost and high performance electrode material was synthesized from porous carbon using thermal treatment using coal-based green needle coke [158]. The porous carbon was thermally treated at 750 °C to deliver better double layer capacitance. The porous carbon material revealed high specific capacitance (274.9 F g−1 at 1 A g−1), and ideal coulomb efficiency (98.9% after 1000 cycles at 5 A g−1) of the sample observes in 3-electrode testing systems. The carbon-based electrode exhibited higher electrochemical performance due to high conductivity and free diffusion pathways and wider potential window 0–1.8 V using in 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte under two-electrode configuration for testing following a button like design, supported by Ni-foam. The supercapacitor conveys a high capacitance of 147.7 F g−1, capacitive retention rate of 75.8% at 12 A g−1, and a prominent energy density of 20.51 Wh kg−1 at power density of 1031.42 W kg−1. Additionally the fabricated supercapacitor demonstrates outstanding capacity retention of 95.6% and Coulombic efficiency of 98.5% after running 5000 cycles at 1 A g−1 thereby depicting better cycling stability.

The utilization of energy has a negligible or minimal negative impact on the environment; social and economic aspects have been termed green energy like solar, biomass, wind, geothermal, and other renewable options. The concept of green energy has evolved to reduce the negative impact of hydrocarbon energy resources, especially their emissions in the form of greenhouse gases. The option of green energy provides a clean and sustainable energy alternative to meet the demands for commercial and non-commercial applications, thereby implying a significant factor in the development of sustainable energy sources and world stability.

The contribution of green energy to the world has led to innovations in terms of technological, industrial, and energy security concerns arising in current times. The increasing shift towards green energy has significantly led to social and economic growth. Generally, the supply and use of green energy are derived from low-cost resources that significantly impact global stability. The stability is portrayed in terms of the central role played by energy in technological and industrial developments to enhance the living stands of the people globally.

Currently, the most critical issues requiring significant attention are energy security and global warming (primarily due to CO2 emissions from fossil fuels) [159–162]. The extensive use and dependence on fossil fuels to meet energy requirements have raised dangerous and severe environmental impacts on human health and welfare. In order to reduce the harmful consequences of fossil fuels, green energy sources and technology require increased exploration. The prime concern for the development of green energy alternatives is environmental compatibility. Based on this characteristic, green energy sources are portrayed as the most promising and attractive energy sources in the future, marking the most promising technological innovations in the context of sustainability.

To achieve sustainability in green energy sources, many factors play a vital role in achieving the prime requirement for green energy to deliver an optimum level in sustainability based on effective and efficient production and usage. The sustainability of energy sources requires reliability and availability at a reasonable cost and minimal negative impact. In this context, fossil fuels lack sustainability features due to limited availability. The low-cost feature of energy resources is also equally essential to increase sustainability.

The abundance and low-cost features of green energy sources can provide a feasible alternative to solve energy scarcity and a solution to environmental consequences. However, long-term actions are the need of the hour to develop green energy resources. The implementation and technological advances in green energy resources provide one of the most efficient and effective approaches toward the rapid development of sustainable energy options in terms of electrochemical devices like supercapacitors. The development of sustainable green energy-based technologies is portrayed semantically in Fig. 23 .

The means under which the environmental concerns raised due to non-sustainable energy sources can be tackled are.
•
Exploration and utilization of renewable technologies and resources.
•
Efficient energy usage along with energy conservation.
•
Switching of energy sources from fossil fuels.
•
Continuous monitoring and evaluation of the energy indicators.
•
The implication of programs based on recycle-reuse-reduce of energy resources.
•
Application of green material options instead of conventional sources.
•
Awareness regarding the use of green and clean energy options to ensure their rapid exploration and development.

Energy consumption in terms of thermo dynamical balance has been an integral part of modern civilization for the comfort and convenience of human life. The amount of energy consumption has provided an indicator for the living standards of modern civilization and the extent of industrialization. Excessive energy utilization has been recognized to produce an adverse impact on the environment thereby disturbing the local and global ecosystem. However, it is worth mentioning that energy consumption from different sources produces diverse environmental impacts. In this context, the impact of fossil fuel consumption has been recognized and well-understood for decades.

On the other hand, renewable energy utilization is considered to impose minimal to negligible impact on the environment. However, it suffers from the extent of its usage and production. Consequently, the negligible consequences of excessive use of bioenergy have been recognized recently in terms of environmental impact and economic concerns attributed to the hike in food prices worldwide over the last decade. Thus it is essential to investigate the composition of energy devices like green supercapacitors and their impact on the environment before their applicability or commercialization.

The development of green supercapacitors, keeping in view the above-mentioned discussion in mind, requires five interconnected components viz.
•
The raw energy source (green materials).
•
Low cost and abundance in nature.
•
Efficient means for storage.
•
Efficient and balanced utilization thermodynamically.
•
Convenient energy distribution (termed as energy carriers or currencies).
•
The extraction and processing of energy systems to produce reasonable energy carriers like supercapacitors is equally important prior to their applicability.

Considering all these factors and applying them in the development of supercapacitors can provide an efficient means for charge storage. However, these supercapacitor devices must employ strict conditions requiring sustainability featuring green materials for different supercapacitor components.

The green supercapacitor fabrication as clean and sustainable energy alternative must feature eco-friendliness, low-cost materials, and renewability, thereby creating less harmful waste and less energy consumption for production.

The supercapacitors feature in green energy technological systems while undergoing fabrication must encompass electrode, electrolyte, separator and current collector materials procured from bio-energetic materials like bio-waste, cellulose, green polymer nanocomposites, etc. The conversion of bio-waste into different forms of carbon presents a suitable approach to the development of green energy systems. The sustainability of such materials dedicated to supercapacitors depends on the material conversion. Significant improvement can be attained regarding supercapacitor sustainability by applying low-cost material conversion methods. Not only does the production cost of supercapacitor components matter in this context, but the material cost also plays a vital role in device sustainability. The bio-waste is rising globally with every passing year, lacking proper disposal or recyclability on a larger scale. Thus, it requires proper strategic planning to employ bio-waste in supercapacitor devices using green energy technology.

To summarize the perspective towards the development of green supercapacitors, low cost, eco-friendliness, less energy consumption, and low waste production stay pivotal to achieve significant results in green energy systems for future generations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges and future trends in green supercapacitors
ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors find their applicability in industry, transportation, military, electronics, and communication due to their excellent characteristics. However, supercapacitors yet lack perfection and exhibit few shortcomings. The shortcomings of supercapacitors present various challenges in their ideal performance delivery. The challenging aspects of green supercapacitors arise due to some technical problems, model establishments, industrial standardization and rate of consistency. Supercapacitors are devices tendering high power density but suffer from low energy density. While batteries yield energy density in the range of 130–200 Wh Kg−1, Supercapacitors deliver energy density <20 Wh Kg−1. Thus improving the energy density to narrow the gap between batteries and supercapacitors is one of the prime challenges.

Currently, most supercapacitors research is based on improving energy density parameters. The enhancement can be attained by improving the manufacturing process and technologies. The improvement in manufacturing technology presents a practical approach to enhance supercapacitors' energy storage capacity. However, the improvement in manufacturing techniques can produce short-term results. Exploring new electrolyte materials and electrodes capable of delivering higher electrochemical performance for long-term results is essential. The supercapacitors exhibiting low energy density result in the development of bulkier devices resulting in the non-compact nature of the equipment. The bulkier nature is due to the ample space occupied by supercapacitor systems to fulfill the required energy demand of the device required for its smooth functioning. The energy density parameter of the supercapacitor can be enhanced by increasing the effective surface area of the materials designed for electrodes. The parameter can also be enhanced by widening or increasing the operating potential. The improvements in these two aspects combined or singularly can enhance energy density to a significant extent. Nowadays, more research for developing green supercapacitors is dedicated to establishing normal materials with a high surface area and their compatibility with different organic electrolytes to enhance energy density. On proper exploration, these two aspects (high surface area and operating potential) may provide efficient supercapacitors comparable enough with batteries in terms of energy density.

Based on the applicability, supercapacitor ideal model is also the primary requirement. For some applications, the supercapacitor model may be portrayed as an ideal model. However, for applications based on power supply, the non-ideal model may result in catastrophic failures. The resonance caused by signals caused by devices along with filters and supercapacitors requires a permanent solution based on the limited energy. Although supercapacitors exhibit the capacity to deliver instantaneous throughput of energy due to high energy, it becomes essential for any supercapacitor to have an ideal design to integrate impacts on load, fluctuations, environment, and sustainability.

The demand for renewable/green energy sources has spiked up globally thereby attaining tremendous attention from the research community over the past few decades. In this context, the development and amelioration of new green materials stays central to push the drive for clean and green energy through renewable sources. In particular, the energy storing materials derived from the waste (bio-waste) has been constantly gaining attention for the development of green supercapacitor technology. The upliftment of such technology is however dependent on consideration of performance parameters and storage mechanisms for various types of supercapacitors like the pore size distribution, energy/power density elevation and cyclic stability on the basis of applicability. In addition to that, the importance of electrode processing and optimization techniques to maximize the performance cannot be neglected. Consideration of the mentioned parameters specifically for the development of green supercapacitors from biowaste provides advantages like waste management; by conversion of waste materials into a useful product along with economic boost for the sustainability of green supercapacitor technology.

For supercapacitors, the rated voltage is very low (<2.7 V), requiring many series connections for practical usage. In devices, supercapacitor requires high charging and discharging current; overcharging usage produces serious consequences for capacitors. Thus for supercapacitors, it is essential to ensure consistency regarding voltage, whether used individually or in series. Additionally, supercapacitors are fabricated within a short time for industrial use. The fabrication of supercapacitors on an industrial scale has been diverse with respect to the industry fabricating them. Since supercapacitors are energy-storing devices and hence require healthy industrial development. The development of supercapacitors on an industrial scale thus requires market consideration to formulate applicable industrial standards. The establishment of industry standards in model classification, electrical test performance analysis, safety concerns, eco-friendliness, general specification, component material specifications, charging and discharging specification, technical requirements for production, transportation, recyclability, etc. In general, developing green supercapacitors on an industrial scale requires storage management, disposal modules, recycling, the interaction of electrode-electrolyte, and processing diaphragm to attain the goal of low cost and renewability. Thus, it becomes prominent to establish and promote healthy industrial development to fabricate next-generation green supercapacitors.

The futuristic approach to developing green supercapacitors requires considering a few technical aspects. The rapid development of wearable and portable devices requires flexible energy storage equipment. The requirement for flexible energy storing devices thus has been trending among researchers. The development of green supercapacitors based on the concept of hybridization provides an effective approach to enhance the energy storage of supercapacitors. Supercapacitors, since their exploration, have been promising candidates for energy storage. Their continuous exploration has established a secure place for them among different electrochemical devices over the years. The evolution of electric transportation has further enhanced its role in the upcoming years. Thus, continuous exploration and research dedication is required to fabricate green supercapacitors to mark their presence and strong candidature in futuristic technologies.

Supercapacitors not only serve as energy alternatives for diverse applications but also serve the purpose of clean, renewable, and green energy storing and delivering devices. Supercapacitors fill the void between conventional capacitors and batteries. The fast charging and discharging kinetics put supercapacitors at the epitome of exploration for futuristic applications. Recently, a shift in paradigm has been observed in terms of development of next generation electrochemical energy storing devices. In particular, the development of supercapacitors, synthesized following green routes and using green materials marks its importance in present research and development. The ideology behind utilization and development of green energy storing devices is to reduce the carcinogenic waste produced by the non green energy storing technologies. The development of green supercapacitors presents a strong alternative for electrochemical energy storage to fulfill the energy storage and harvesting requirements for the next generation electronic devices including the hybrid electric vehicles. This review portrays an attempt towards the development of such green supercapacitors, considering the design and green energy perspective along with their importance as futuristic energy devices. As the motto of modern times goes cleaner, green energy is the way forward. Clean and green energy sources with high sustainability may serve the following generation energy requirements. Hence, exploring green and sustainable energy storage devices to their fullest potential is essential.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We,hereby declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
